summary: Patrick McKenzie argues that micro-ISV developers should aggressively outsource tasks that aren't core to their business. He presents four heuristics for deciding what to outsource: when customers can't tell the difference, when the cost is trivially low, when outsourced quality exceeds what you could produce, and when the effective hourly rate of doing it yourself is insultingly low. He illustrates with examples from his own Bingo Card Creator business, including paying $5/month for payment notification handling and $29.95 for professional stock icons. He describes a 'cookie jar' technique where you physically pay yourself per hour to internalize the opportunity cost of DIY work on low-value tasks.
key_points: Your time as a solo developer is your scarcest resource — spend it on what makes your business special, not on commoditized tasks
Four outsourcing heuristics: customer-invisible work, trivially cheap services, quality improvements, and low implied hourly rate
Never outsource customer-facing support or core product logic — those are your competitive advantages as a small business
Programmers have a psychological blind spot where they'll tolerate weeks of wheel-reinvention but can't stand 15 seconds of repetitive nuisance
The 'cookie jar trick' of physically paying yourself per hour makes the opportunity cost of low-value work viscerally real
money_quotes: My question is what you'd have to be smoking to want to.
For things you are less good at, outsource them. Now.
The cost of outsourcing is lower than the cost to even consider whether you're capable of insourcing.
Have you ever noticed that computer programmers can't tolerate 15 seconds of nuisance an hour but will happily spend man-weeks reinventing the wheel?
I wouldn't outsource programming the key features of my program. But everything else is fair game.
I'm not an artist — the one thing in my life I have ever successfully drawn is a goomba.
After 1,000 yen was in the cup I surrendered and just downloaded this guy's solution.
The 1,000 yen stupidity tax is lunch today. I'm thinking chicken.
key_insight: Solo software developers systematically undervalue their own time and should outsource any task where the cost is lower than the opportunity cost of doing it themselves.
